title: Share of artificial intelligence jobs among all job postings
subtitle: A job posting is considered an AI job if it requests one or more AI skills, e.g., "natural language processing", "neural networks", "machine learning", or "robotics".
caption:
Share of artificial intelligence jobs among all job postings

A job posting is considered an AI job if it requests one or more AI skills, e.g., "natural language processing", "neural networks", "machine learning", or "robotics".

Across nine countries between 2014 and 2024, the share of job postings requesting artificial intelligence skills varies by nation and generally rises over time, measured as a percentage on the vertical axis ranging from 0% to 1.8%. The United States line begins near 0.6% in 2014, climbs steadily to roughly 1.4% by 2018, dips slightly around 2020, then ascends to approximately 1.7% in 2022 before reaching 1.8% in 2024 — the highest value shown for any country in any year. The United Kingdom starts near 0.4% in 2014, rises to about 0.9% by 2019, peaks near 1.35% in 2022, then falls to around 1.25% in 2024. Sweden’s line begins near 0.9% in 2019, climbs to 1.25% in 2021, dips to 1.05% in 2023, then rises again to 1.3% in 2024. The Netherlands, Spain, and Austria all begin their visible traces around 2019–2020 between 0.6% and 0.8%, rise to cluster near 1.1% to 1.35% by 2022, then converge again near 1.05% to 1.25% in 2024. Italy’s line starts near 0.5% in 2019, rises to 0.7% in 2020, dips to 0.6% in 2022, then climbs to 0.85% in 2024. New Zealand remains at the bottom throughout, starting near 0.15% in 2014, hovering below 0.2% until 2019, then rising gradually to about 0.55% in 2024. The United States consistently occupies the top position across all years where data is available, while New Zealand remains at the lowest level across the entire period.
